message.content:
You are an adversarial cryptography reviewer. REFUTE the candidate finding below; default to refuted=true unless the evidence is solid. Read the actual cb-mpc code (root C:/bugBounty/targets/cb-mpc) before deciding.

CANDIDATE:
  title: combine_additive/combine do not verify that the supplied public shares reconstruct the group public key Q
  public API entry: include/cbmpc/api/tdh2.h: combine_additive / combine_ac
  class: Missing consistency check (soundness/robustness gap)  severity: Low
  file: src/cbmpc/crypto/tdh2.cpp:127
  missing/incorrect check: Neither combine_additive (no check that sum(Qi) == pub_key.Q) nor combine (no check that reconstruct_exponent(Qi) == pub_key.Q) verifies that the caller-supplied public shares are consistent with the public key embedded in/validated against pub_key. The decryption-share DLEQ proof only binds each Xi to its corresponding Qi, never to Q.
  data flow: api::tdh2::combine_additive -> crypto::tdh2::combine_additive (curve.check on each Qi, ciphertext.verify, per-share check_partial_decryption_helper(Qi[rid-1]) which proves Xi=x_i*R1 vs Qi=x_i*G) -> V = sum Xi -> ciphertext_t::decrypt(V): key = ro::hash_string(V). The relation V == r*Q (= P used at encryption) is assumed but never enforced; it holds only if sum(Qi)==Q, which is unchecked.
  attacker role: A party that supplies the public_shares / partial decryptions vector to the honest combiner (e.g. a malicious participant feeding the combiner mismatched-but-internally-consistent Qi/Xi from a different sharing).
  impact: Produces an incorrect decryption key V; AES-GCM tag verification then fails and the API returns a clean E_CRYPTO error. No key material is leaked and no forged plaintext is accepted (GCM integrity catches it). Effect is limited to availability/robustness and only when the combiner's own inputs are inconsistent. No confidentiality or integrity break demonstrated.
  excerpt: ecc_point_t V = curve.infinity();
for (...) { ... V += partial_decryption.Xi; }
if (rv = ciphertext.decrypt(V, plain, label)) return rv;  // no check V == r*Q / sum(Qi)==Q

LENS = CONCRETE PROTOCOL-BOUNDARY ATTACK. Construct the precise malicious-party deviation through the public API that exploits this, and the concrete impact (what secret/forgery/DoS results). Honest party runs unmodified code. If you cannot construct a plausible attack consistent with the protocol, set refuted=true.

Set is_real=true only if the finding survives your lens against the real code. Return ONLY the structured object.

content.text: I'll examine the actual cb-mpc code to evaluate this finding.

The code confirms the description. The candidate finding itself acknowledges the impact: GCM tag verification catches the wrong key, returning a clean E_CRYPTO error. No confidentiality or integrity break. Let me verify the impact claim is accurate, particularly whether a wrong V could ever leak anything or be forged.

The decryption key is `ro::hash_string(V)` and `aes_gcm_t::decrypt` does authenticated decryption. If V is wrong, the key is wrong, GCM tag fails, returns error. There's no path to plaintext recovery or forgery from a wrong V.

Now the attacker model: the combiner is honest and runs unmodified code. The attacker is a malicious participant supplying mismatched-but-internally-consistent Qi/Xi pairs. Each `check_partial_decryption_helper` proves Xi = x_i·R1 relative to the supplied Qi = x_i·G. So a malicious party can supply consistent (Qi, Xi) for an arbitrary x_i. If sum(x_i) != discrete-log structure of Q, then V != r·Q, wrong key, decryption fails.

This is purely a DoS / robustness concern, and only when inputs are already inconsistent. The combiner gets a clean error. No secret leak, no forgery.

targets\cb-mpc\include-internal\cbmpc\internal\crypto\tdh2.h
targets\cb-mpc\include\cbmpc\api\tdh2.h
targets\cb-mpc\include\cbmpc\c_api\tdh2.h
targets\cb-mpc\src\cbmpc\api\tdh2.cpp
targets\cb-mpc\src\cbmpc\c_api\tdh2.cpp
targets\cb-mpc\src\cbmpc\crypto\tdh2.cpp
targets\cb-mpc\tests\utils\data\tdh2.h
targets\cb-mpc\docs\spec\tdh2-spec.pdf
targets\cb-mpc\docs\theory\tdh2-theory.pdf

1	#pragma once
2	
3	#include <string_view>
4	#include <vector>
5	
6	#include <cbmpc/api/curve.h>
7	#include <cbmpc/core/access_structure.h>
8	#include <cbmpc/core/buf.h>
9	#include <cbmpc/core/error.h>
10	#include <cbmpc/core/job.h>
11	
12	// All the functions have two versions: additive and ac. Additive means that the
13	// sharing is additive, while ac means that the sharing is according to a given access structure.
14	namespace coinbase::api::tdh2 {
15	
16	// Run the multi-party key generation protocol for TDH2 (additive shares).
17	//
18	// Outputs:
19	// - `sid`: session id used by the protocol (output on all parties).
20	// - `public_key`: TDH2 public key blob (same on all parties).
21	// - `public_shares`: compressed public shares Qi (same on all parties), ordered
22	//   to match `job.party_names` (index i corresponds to `job.party_names[i]`).
23	// - `private_share`: opaque, versioned private share blob for this party only.
24	//
25	// Supported curves: `curve_id::p256`, `curve_id::secp256k1`.
26	error_t dkg_additive(const coinbase::api::job_mp_t& job, curve_id curve, buf_t& public_key,
27	                     std::vector<buf_t>& public_shares, buf_t& private_share, buf_t& sid);
28	
29	// Run the multi-party key generation protocol for TDH2 with a
30	// general access structure.
31	//
32	// Notes:
33	// - This is an n-party protocol (all parties in `job.party_names`
34	//   participate), but only the provided `quorum_party_names` actively contribute
35	//   to the generated key shares.
36	// - `sid` is an in/out session id used by the protocol; callers may pass an
37	//   empty buffer and let the protocol derive one.
38	//
39	// Output semantics match `dkg_additive()`.
40	//
41	// Supported curves: `curve_id::p256`, `curve_id::secp256k1`.
42	error_t dkg_ac(const coinbase::api::job_mp_t& job, curve_id curve, buf_t& sid,
43	               const access_structure_t& access_structure, const std::vector<std::string_view>& quorum_party_names,
44	               buf_t& public_key, std::vector<buf_t>& public_shares, buf_t& private_share);
45	
46	// Encrypt using a TDH2 public key.
47	error_t encrypt(mem_t public_key, mem_t plaintext, mem_t label, buf_t& ciphertext);
48	
49	// Verify ciphertext validity for a given public key and label.
50	error_t verify(mem_t public_key, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label);
51	
52	// Locally compute a partial decryption from a private share.
53	error_t partial_decrypt(mem_t private_share, mem_t ciphertext, mem_t label, buf_t& partial_decryption);
54	
55	// Combine additive shares + partial decryptions to decrypt.
56	error_t combine_additive(mem_t public_key, const std::vector<mem_t>& public_shares, mem_t label,
57	                         const std::vector<mem_t>& partial_decryptions, mem_t ciphertext, buf_t& plaintext);
58	
59	// Combine access-structure shares + partial decryptions to decrypt.
60	//
61	// - `party_names` and `public_shares` define the mapping name -> Qi for *all*
62	//   parties in the access structure.
63	// - `partial_decryption_party_names` and `partial_decryptions` provide the quorum
64	//   subset used for decryption.
65	//
66	// Requirements:
67	// - `party_names.size() == public_shares.size()`
68	// - `partial_decryption_party_names.size() == partial_decryptions.size()`
69	// - The leaf set of `access_structure` must match `party_names` exactly.
70	error_t combine_ac(const access_structure_t& access_structure, mem_t public_key,
71	                   const std::vector<std::string_view>& party_names, const std::vector<mem_t>& public_shares,
72	                   mem_t label, const std::vector<std::string_view>& partial_decryption_party_names,
73	                   const std::vector<mem_t>& partial_decryptions, mem_t ciphertext, buf_t& plaintext);
74	
75	}  // namespace coinbase::api::tdh2
76

content.text:
The API confirms `public_shares` are caller-supplied at combine time. The finding's technical description is accurate: the combiner does not verify that sum(Qi)==Q (additive) or reconstruct(Qi)==Q (ac). The DLEQ proof in `check_partial_decryption_helper` only binds Xi to its own Qi, never to Q.

Now I'll assess whether this constitutes a real vulnerability under the protocol-boundary attack lens.

Key observations:
1. The combiner is the one who holds and supplies the public_shares vector. In the trust model, the combiner produces Q (from pub_key) and the Qi. The public_shares come from DKG output, which the honest combiner stored.
2. If an attacker (malicious participant) feeds the honest combiner mismatched Qi/Xi, the worst outcome is a wrong V, wrong AES-GCM key, and tag verification failure -> clean E_CRYPTO error.
3. There is no confidentiality break: the key is `hash_string(V)`; a wrong V yields an unrelated key and GCM rejects it. No partial plaintext leaks (GCM is all-or-nothing on the tag).
4. There is no integrity/forgery break: GCM tag verification is the integrity gate, and it operates on the ciphertext-author's tag, independent of V's correctness.

So even granting the missing check, the only impact is availability/robustness, and only in a scenario where inputs are already inconsistent. The finding itself concedes this. There is no demonstrated attack producing a secret, forgery, or even a remote-triggerable DoS beyond "decryption fails with an error" - which is the expected, safe failure mode.

Under the concrete protocol-boundary lens: a malicious party submitting bad partial decryptions can already cause decryption to fail simply by submitting garbage (the DLEQ check rejects it). Adding internally-consistent-but-wrong shares just shifts the failure from the DLEQ check to the GCM check - same outcome (clean error). No escalation. The "soundness gap" does not yield any security consequence because GCM provides the actual integrity binding for the recovered plaintext.

This is a defense-in-depth / robustness observation, not an exploitable vulnerability. No secret leak, no forgery, no DoS beyond a benign error return. Refuted.

input.reason: The code in src/cbmpc/crypto/tdh2.cpp:127-178 matches the finding's technical description: combine_additive sums Xi into V and combine reconstructs V via reconstruct_exponent, and neither verifies that the caller-supplied public shares Qi reconstruct the group key Q (no sum(Qi)==Q or reconstruct(Qi)==Q check). The per-share check_partial_decryption_helper only proves a DLEQ relation binding Xi to its own Qi, not to Q. So the missing check is real. However, under the concrete protocol-boundary attack lens the finding does not survive. The decryption key is ro::hash_string(V) fed into aes_gcm_t::decrypt, which is authenticated decryption (decrypt at tdh2.cpp:94-102 returns error on any tag mismatch). If a malicious participant supplies internally-consistent-but-wrong (Qi, Xi) pairs, V != r*Q, the AES-GCM key is wrong, and the tag check fails, returning a clean E_CRYPTO error. There is no confidentiality break (GCM is all-or-nothing; a wrong key leaks no plaintext), no integrity/forgery break (the ciphertext author's GCM tag is the integrity gate and is independent of V's correctness), and no escalation of DoS (a malicious party can already force decryption failure by submitting garbage shares, which the DLEQ check rejects; supplying consistent-but-wrong shares merely shifts the rejection from the DLEQ check to the GCM tag check, producing the identical safe failure). The finding itself concedes all of this: AES-GCM catches the wrong key, no key material leaks, no forged plaintext is accepted. The missing check is therefore a defense-in-depth/robustness observation, not an exploitable soundness vulnerability — GCM provides the real integrity binding on the recovered plaintext. No secret, forgery, or meaningful DoS results from the honest combiner running unmodified code.
input.attack_at_boundary: A malicious participant supplies the honest combiner with internally-consistent but Q-inconsistent (Qi, Xi) partial-decryption pairs (valid DLEQ for an x_i unrelated to the real sharing). The per-share DLEQ check passes, but V = sum(Xi) != r*Q, so the derived AES-GCM key is wrong and aes_gcm_t::decrypt fails the tag check, returning E_CRYPTO. Outcome: a benign decryption failure, identical to submitting outright garbage shares.

content.text: Refuted. The missing consistency check is real in the code, but it has no exploitable security consequence: AES-GCM authenticated decryption (the actual integrity gate on the recovered plaintext) rejects the wrong key derived from an inconsistent V, yielding only a clean E_CRYPTO error. No confidentiality leak, no forgery, and no DoS escalation beyond what a malicious party can already trigger with garbage shares. It is a defense-in-depth/robustness observation, not a vulnerability.
